system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 5: Jazz Standard Study #4: It Could Happen to You
Pages 70-86 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

It Could Happen to You is an excellent jazz standard to study because
it covers so many different aspects of diatonic harmony. It just about
hits every chord in the diatonic series.

That’s why | think this jazz standard is a perfect next step in our stud-
ies. In All of Me we started to see how harmony can be analyzed in dif-
ferent ways, and while that still rings true for It Could Happen to You,
this tune stays reasonably predictable.

It Could Happen te You Bio

It Could Happen to You was written by Jimmy Van Heusen with lyrics by Johnny
Burke. It was first introduced in the 1943 musical comedy And the Angel Sings.
It was most commonly played in the key of concert Eb major.

It Could Happen to You has a 32 bar form. This is another great one to
learn the lyrics to. It has a simple, tasteful melody, and | find that the
lyrics really stick out.

Here are some recordings | suggest.

>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Frank Sinatra from “Close to You” 1957

Chet Baker from “Chet Baker Sings” 1958

Miles Davis from “Relaxin” 1956

Sonny Clark from “Dial S for Sonny” 1957



There are many other great versions of this song. Make sure you inves-
tigate further than just these suggestions.

You'll notice that the Frank Sinatra and Sonny Clark versions play It
Could Happen to You as a ballad. Typically, this tune is performed at
a medium tempo, but this song is very versatile and can be played in
any way you would like. This is certainly something that | enjoy about
this song.

In addition to this, sometimes it can be helpful to learn a song by lis-
tening to a ballad version if it is available. This way you can hear it
slowed down and stripped down.

Let’s go ahead and move into our study. As usual, you will see the chord
map, guide tone chart, chords analysis, and then I'll talk about it.

Let’s start by defining the color-coding.
Blue = directly related to the parent key center.
‘Yellow = ii-V of ii.
[Purple = ii-V of iii.
Green = temporary shift to minor iv.

Red = ii-V of vi.

The first thing | want to point out about this chord analysis is there are
a lot of ii-V’s of diatonic chords. Scan through, and you will see ii-V of
il, ii-V of ili, and ii-V of vi.

Remember in our last study how we talked about secondary domi-

nants? Well, it’s just as easy to add a ii chord in front of it to create a
ii-V chord progression. This is precisely what is happening here.

Let’s take a look at that very first line.

( Eom? ) (Gon? )
E?was” Gun7? C7) Egy? Amn? 79
I ii-V of ii ii ii-V of iii
(New Key Center) (Key OF G Minor - No Resolution)

| want to talk about target chords for a second. A target chord is sim-
ply a chord within a progression that’s being targeted. In a ii-V-| chord
progression, the target chord is the | chord. The ii and V are targeting
the |, the arrival point.

So which diatonic chords are being targeted in this first line? We start
with the | chord to establish the tonal center. Then we have a ii-V of ii,
which is targeting the ii chord. Then we have a ii-V of iii, which does not
resolve to the iii but still anticipates a movement.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



Remember, the most important thing to pay attention to when trying
to understand the harmony of a song is where the chords are going.
If we look at a Gmin7(b5) and a C7(b9) as separate chords, they are
confusing. But if we look at them as resolving to an Fmin7, then we
have something functional to understand. Then we can look at the
Fmin7 and ask how it functions in the overall tonality of the song.

In our study of All of Me, the angle we took for understanding ii-V’s
of diatonic chords, was thinking of them as approaching different key
centers. This is still an accurate way to think about this, but it’s only
part of the story. We have to look at how the target chords function
within the overall harmonic movement.

Let’s take a look at the next line.

( APway7 )
EP was? A” Gun?” c7
. I IV7 li-V of ii --------------------

(Or iii(b5)-VI709))

If we want to relate the ii-V of ii directly to the parent tonal center,
then what would those chords represent? The Gmin7(b5) can also be
thought of as the iiib5 (adding a b5 to the iii minor) and a VI7.

A dominant VI, as we’ve discussed before is not at all uncommon. On
top of that, dominant chords are easy to alter, so ab? makes perfect
sense considering the chord it is leading to. A illb5 is odd, but again,
if we combine this understanding with the ii-V of ii concept everything
ties together.

Let’s discuss one more line.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



(B’” )

Fun? APwine EPaas7 Dwin7) G70
7 ii Minor iv I ii-V of vi
(Key OF C Minor)

In bar 10 there are two common sets of chord progressions. The most
basic way to approach it is with a V chord (Bb7) leading into the |. But
often times, musicians will play an Abmin6, which functions as the mi-
nor IV chord. In our All of Me study, we talked about how you can per-
ceive the minor tv as a quick jump into the minor tonic key.

You can also turn that Abminé into a ii-V (Abmin7-Db7), or you can
even just play a Db7, which | do in some upcoming examples.

In bar 12 we have a classic ii-V of vi which leads into the relative minor.
The rest of the form is either repeats of sections we just covered or
cut and dry functional diatonic harmony.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK | 80 |



IMPROV LESSON
Vractice Composing Melodies

In our Blue Bossa study, we talked about the importance of the mel-
ody and how we can use it to inform our improvisation. And though
we took a quick detour to discuss using chord tones in our last improv
lesson, | want to come back to this concept of melody.

As I've already stressed, the ultimate goal in our jazz improvisation
is to create great melodies in the moment. The goal isn’t to play the
most technical, virtuosic lines or the flashiest patterns. If you can do
so while creating great melodies, awesome, but that shouldn’t be your
primary focus.

But how do we learn to create great melodies?

A considerable part of this is listening. The more you listen to jazz and
solos you like, the more you will start to understand the language. The
more melodies of jazz standards or other kinds of songs you listen to
and learn, the more you will widen your musical vocabulary.

But there are some excellent practices for working on this and one of
my favorites is to create contrafacts.

>> WHAT'S A CONTRAFACT?

A contrafact is a melody that is written over top of an existing songs
chord changes, thus creating a new song altogether. Classic examples
of this are rhythm changes heads like “Oleo” or “Anthropology.” Those
are melodies written over the changes to Gershwin’'s “| Got Rhythm.”



You can think of contrafacts as simply borrowing the chord changes of
another song. Not a bad way to compose a song, right? You already
like the chord progression to an existing song, just write a new melody.

Why does writing contrafacts help with improvising melodies?

Improvising is often described as “composition sped up.” But if you
want to compose quickly, wouldn't it be helpful to practice composing
slowly (aka a normal pace)?

When we take the time to compose a melody, we are allowing our-
selves to dream up exactly what we want to express. Composition in
many ways is the ideal environment for creating melodies, not impro-
visation. But when we hear some of the best jazz musicians play it
seems as if they have an endless flow of extraordinary musical ideas.
We want to compose melodies at that speed and at that level.

So this improv lesson requires that you take action. The assignment is
to create a contrafact over the chord changes of It Could Happen
to You.

This doesn’t mean you have to notate it, although that can be a great
idea. But certainly compose it on your instrument, memorize it, and if
you can, record it so you can keep it logged away.

Of course, I’m not going to leave you hanging! I’m going to share two
different examples of contrafacts I’ve written over these chord chang-
es. Both are different in their own way, and I'll explain that.

The first one I’m cleverly calling “It Just Happened.” Take a look, lis-
ten to the recording, and play through it yourself.


IT JUST HAPPENED
Brent Vaartstra
Recorded 8va
E♭MAJ7
GMIN7(b5) C7(b9) FMIN7 AMIN7(b5) D7(b9)
E♭MAJ7 A♭7 GMIN7(b5) C7(#9)
FMIN7 D♭7(#11) E♭MAJ7 DMIN7(b5) G7(b9)
CMIN7 F7 FMIN7 B♭7(b9)
E♭MAJ7 GMIN7(b5) C7(b9) FMIN7 AMIN7(b5) D7(b9)
E♭MAJ7 A♭7 GMIN7(b5) C7(#9)
FMIN7 D♭7(#11) E♭MAJ7 GMIN7(b5) C7(b9)
FMIN7 B♭7(b9) E♭7
THE JAZZ STANDARDS

For this contrafact, | tried to keep the melody as simple as | could. My
goal was to create a sing-able melody. This is the kind of contrafact
| suggest you compose. Try to keep it simple. The exercise here is to
create a strong melody that both outlines the chord changes appro-
priately while expressing a theme.

Keep in mind, if you’re new to this sort of thing, don’t worry. Your melo-
dy doesn’t have to be perfect. The only way to get better at compos-
ing and improvising is to keep doing it. So take your best shot at it, |
promise it will be worth it!

A couple things to note in my contrafact:

1. There are re-occurring rhythmic and melodic themes through-
out with variations. These are good characteristics for any song
to have, but ultimately you will want some of this in your improv
too.

2. | leave space. There is room for the melody to breathe, and
there also should be in your improvisation.

| want to share with you one more contrafact. This one is different than
the last in that it is in the Bebop style. In case you aren’t familiar, Be-
bop is a style in jazz developed in the 1940’s by pioneers like Charlie
Parker and Dizzy Gillespie. A lot of contrafacts were written in this era,
and they tend to be busier, less sing-able and similar to a solo.

Take a look, listen, and then play through it for yourself.

Now, if you want to write a contrafact like this, you will probably need
a decent understanding of jazz language and how to navigate chord
progressions. This isn’t easy to do if you don’t have this experience, so
don’t feel pressured or intimidated if you aren’t there yet! But definite-
ly, have fun playing through this.

This may even seem to you like a full-on bebop solo, as is the nature
of bebop heads, but note the themes throughout. There are repeated
sections, rhythmic clones and general structure. So, if you do go this
route with your contrafact, make sure you include all of these elements.

Writing a contrafact over It Could Happen to You will not only help you
improve your improvisation in general, it will help you understand how
to play this jazz standard.

It Could Happen to You has so many essential elements that you will
find over and over again in jazz standards, so | encourage you to take
your time and review this chapter if needed.

Let’s move on to our next study: Sweet Georgia Brown.


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
